title: Supabase Backend — Schema, RLS, and Client Init
deliverable: Four Postgres tables created in Supabase with RLS enabled and per-row user_id policies; src/lib/supabase.js initialises the JS client from env vars; .env.example documents required vars.

in: Create the Supabase project (manual step, documented)
SQL DDL for all four tables: profiles, queue_items, chains, done_items — exact columns per PRD §4.3
Enable RLS on every table immediately after table creation
RLS policies for SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE on each table enforcing user_id = auth.uid() (profiles keyed on id = auth.uid())
Policies created before any data operations per PRD §7.4
src/lib/supabase.js — initialise @supabase/supabase-js client from VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
Note on applying migration via Supabase MCP tool or SQL editor
out: Auth providers / magic-link / Google OAuth configuration (Block 17)
Any application code that reads/writes these tables (Blocks 17, 18)
Seeding default chains into the database (Block 18)

files.purpose: Initialise and export the Supabase JS client singleton using VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY
Document the two required environment variables for local and CI/CD setup
Single SQL migration file containing all DDL (CREATE TABLE, RLS enable, policies) — applied via Supabase MCP apply_migration or copy-pasted into the SQL editor

implementation_steps.details: In the Supabase dashboard (app.supabase.com) create a new project named 'niyet'. Choose a region close to Turkey (eu-central-1 or eu-west-1). Copy the Project URL and anon public key — these become VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY. Alternatively use the Supabase MCP tool mcp__plugin_supabase_supabase__create_project if available. Record the project ref for CLI use.
Run: npm install @supabase/supabase-js. Verify package.json lists the dependency. No other packages are needed for this block.

Create .env.example at project root:

  VITE_SUPABASE_URL=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co
  VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=<anon-public-key>

Create a local .env (git-ignored) with real values. Ensure .env is in .gitignore.

implementation_steps.description: Write the migration SQL file — profiles table
Write the migration SQL — queue_items table
Write the migration SQL — chains table
Write the migration SQL — done_items table
Write the migration SQL — handle_new_user() trigger (C3)
implementation_steps.details:
Fifth section of migration file — auto-create profiles row on auth.users INSERT (eliminates profile-creation race; Blocks 17/18 do NOT need to upsert profiles manually):

  -- Auto-create a profiles row whenever a new auth.users row is inserted
  CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION public.handle_new_user()
  RETURNS trigger
  LANGUAGE plpgsql
  SECURITY DEFINER
  SET search_path = public
  AS $$
  BEGIN
    INSERT INTO public.profiles (id, show_count)
    VALUES (NEW.id, 1)
    ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING;
    RETURN NEW;
  END;
  $$;

  CREATE OR REPLACE TRIGGER on_auth_user_created
    AFTER INSERT ON auth.users
    FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION public.handle_new_user();

SECURITY DEFINER is required because the trigger runs in the context of the inserting principal (typically the auth service), not the row owner; it needs elevated rights to write to public.profiles. The ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING guard makes the trigger idempotent in edge-case replays.

Option A — Supabase MCP: call mcp__plugin_supabase_supabase__apply_migration with the full SQL content of supabase/migrations/20260529000000_initial_schema.sql and the project ref obtained in step 16.1.

Option B — SQL editor: open the Supabase dashboard → SQL Editor → paste the full migration SQL → Run.

Option C — CLI: supabase db push (requires supabase CLI and linked project).

Verify in Table Editor that all four tables appear, RLS is toggled ON, and the four policies per table are visible under Authentication → Policies.

data_structures.name: profiles
data_structures.shape: { id: uuid PK (= auth.users.id), created_at: timestamptz default now(), show_count: int2 default 1 }
data_structures.notes: One row per authenticated user. Created at first sign-in by Block 17. show_count stores the user's visible-task preference (1/2/3/5). RLS keyed on id = auth.uid() rather than user_id.

data_structures.name: queue_items
data_structures.shape: { id: uuid PK default gen_random_uuid(), user_id: uuid FK→profiles.id cascade delete, text: text, position: int4 default 0, created_at: timestamptz default now() }
data_structures.notes: Active queue. position is 0-indexed ordering. Multiple items may temporarily share a position during optimistic writes; Block 18 normalises ordering on sync. Cascade delete removes all items when the profile is deleted.

data_structures.name: chains
data_structures.shape: { id: uuid PK default gen_random_uuid(), user_id: uuid FK→profiles.id cascade delete, name: text, emoji: text default '📋', steps: text[] default '{}', position: int4 default 0, is_default: boolean default false, created_at: timestamptz default now(), updated_at: timestamptz default now() }
data_structures.notes: Reusable step sequences. steps is a Postgres text array. is_default=true for the 7 shipped chains seeded at merge-on-login (Block 18). updated_at is maintained by trigger and used by Block 18 as a merge tiebreaker. Cascade delete on profile.

data_structures.name: done_items
data_structures.shape: { id: uuid PK default gen_random_uuid(), user_id: uuid FK→profiles.id cascade delete, text: text, completed_at: timestamptz default now(), chain_id: uuid nullable FK→chains.id ON DELETE SET NULL }
data_structures.notes: Completed task log. chain_id is nullable — set only when the item was loaded from a chain. ON DELETE SET NULL preserves history if the chain is later deleted. Cascade delete on profile.

data_structures.name: SupabaseClient
data_structures.notes: Singleton exported from src/lib/supabase.js. All Supabase calls in the app go through this one instance. No direct instantiation elsewhere.

interfaces.name: supabase (exported singleton)
interfaces.purpose: The single Supabase client instance used app-wide. Imported by Block 17 (auth), Block 18 (sync), and any future direct DB callers. Created once in src/lib/supabase.js from env vars.

acceptance_criteria: All four tables (profiles, queue_items, chains, done_items) exist in the Supabase project public schema with columns exactly matching PRD §4.3.
RLS is enabled on every table — visible in Supabase dashboard Authentication → Policies or via SELECT relrowsecurity FROM pg_class WHERE relname IN ('profiles','queue_items','chains','done_items').
Each table has four policies: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — all scoped to user_id = auth.uid() (profiles: id = auth.uid()).

Policies exist before any data operations — confirmed by migration order within the single SQL file: ALTER TABLE ... ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY immediately follows CREATE TABLE; CREATE POLICY statements follow immediately.
An unauthenticated SELECT on any table returns an empty array or a PGRST301 error — never a cross-user data leak.
src/lib/supabase.js exists, imports createClient from @supabase/supabase-js, reads VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY from import.meta.env, and exports a single supabase constant.
Calling import { supabase } from './lib/supabase.js' in any module resolves to the same client instance without throwing.
.env.example exists at project root documenting both VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY.
.env (with real values) is listed in .gitignore and never committed.
supabase/migrations/20260529000000_initial_schema.sql contains the full DDL (all 4 tables + RLS + policies) as a single idempotent file (IF NOT EXISTS guards).
cascade delete is in place: deleting a profiles row removes all related queue_items, chains, and done_items automatically.
done_items.chain_id uses ON DELETE SET NULL so completed-task history is preserved when a chain is deleted.
verification: In Supabase Table Editor: confirm all four tables appear; confirm column names, types, defaults, and nullability match PRD §4.3 exactly.
In Authentication → Policies: confirm RLS toggle is ON for each table; confirm 4 policies per table with correct USING / WITH CHECK expressions.
Run in SQL editor: SELECT relrowsecurity FROM pg_class WHERE relname IN ('profiles','queue_items','chains','done_items'); — all four rows must return true.
Run in SQL editor (as anon / unauthenticated): SELECT * FROM public.queue_items; — must return 0 rows or permission denied, never another user's rows.
Run: npm run dev — app starts without errors. Open browser console, confirm no 'Missing Supabase env vars' error.

In browser console on running app: const { data, error } = await supabase.from('profiles').select('*').limit(1); console.log(error); — error should be null or a 401/PGRST301 (RLS blocks anonymous), never a network/config error.
Check that import { supabase } from './lib/supabase.js' can be imported in two separate modules and both receive the identical object reference (singleton check).
Verify .env is absent from git tracked files: git ls-files .env — should return nothing.
Verify .env.example is tracked: git ls-files .env.example — should return .env.example.

questionable_decisions.decision: profiles.id is the PK referencing auth.users.id directly rather than having a separate user_id column
questionable_decisions.assumption: This follows the standard Supabase pattern and is explicitly in PRD §4.3; it is cleaner than a redundant user_id column on profiles.
alternatives: Add a separate user_id uuid column to profiles with a UNIQUE constraint (more uniform schema across all tables but redundant)
questionable_decisions.recommendation: Keep id = auth.uid() on profiles per PRD. The RLS policy reads id = auth.uid() rather than user_id = auth.uid() — this must be documented clearly so Block 17/18 implementors don't accidentally write the wrong policy predicate.

questionable_decisions.decision: queue_items FK references public.profiles(id) rather than auth.users(id) directly
questionable_decisions.assumption: Referencing profiles ensures a profile row exists before queue items can be inserted, enforcing the invariant that every authed user has a profile. However it means profile creation must happen at sign-in (Block 17) before any queue writes.
alternatives: FK references auth.users(id) directly — fewer dependencies but loses the profiles existence guarantee
No FK, just a user_id column — loses referential integrity entirely
questionable_decisions.recommendation: Keep FK → profiles(id). Block 17 must upsert the profiles row immediately on sign-in before any queue/chain writes, or queue_item inserts will fail with a foreign key violation. Document this hard dependency.
questionable_decisions.resolution: C3 — locked: DB trigger handle_new_user() (SECURITY DEFINER) auto-creates the profiles row on AFTER INSERT ON auth.users; Blocks 17/18 no longer need to upsert profiles manually.

questionable_decisions.decision: Single monolithic SQL migration file instead of separate per-table migration files
questionable_decisions.assumption: Simpler to apply manually via Supabase SQL editor (one paste, one run). The Supabase MCP apply_migration tool also accepts a single SQL string.
alternatives: Four separate migration files (one per table) — better for rollback granularity
Supabase CLI supabase db push with multiple timestamped migration files — better for team workflows
questionable_decisions.recommendation: Single file is acceptable for a solo project. If the project ever adds a second developer or uses Supabase CLI branching, split into per-table files. Use IF NOT EXISTS guards for idempotency.

questionable_decisions.decision: done_items.chain_id uses ON DELETE SET NULL rather than CASCADE or RESTRICT
questionable_decisions.assumption: PRD says chain_id is nullable and the done log should persist history even if the chain is deleted. ON DELETE SET NULL preserves completed-task history (important for the done log and future analytics) while allowing chain deletion.
alternatives: ON DELETE CASCADE — deletes done_items when chain is deleted; destroys history
ON DELETE RESTRICT — prevents chain deletion if done_items reference it; too strict for UX
questionable_decisions.recommendation: ON DELETE SET NULL is correct per PRD intent. No change needed.

questionable_decisions.decision: chains table does not include an updated_at column
questionable_decisions.assumption: Block 18 (merge-on-login) needs a reliable tiebreaker to resolve conflicts between local and remote chain edits. Without updated_at the only available tiebreaker is created_at, which is immutable and cannot reflect mutations.
alternatives: Use a client-supplied updated_at passed from the app — risks clock skew and intentional manipulation
Use a monotonic version counter (int) incremented on every update — reliable but requires more coordination
questionable_decisions.recommendation: Add updated_at timestamptz default now() to the chains table and maintain it with a BEFORE UPDATE trigger.
questionable_decisions.resolution: C6 — locked by user: chains.updated_at added as merge tiebreaker for Block 18. Column added to CREATE TABLE and a set_chains_updated_at() BEFORE UPDATE trigger maintains it automatically.

questionable_decisions.decision: Anon key exposed in client-side JS (VITE_ prefix means it's bundled into the browser build)
questionable_decisions.assumption: PRD §4.3 explicitly states 'The anon key is safe to expose in the client — RLS enforces isolation.' This is the standard Supabase architecture.
alternatives: Use a Cloudflare Worker / Edge Function as a proxy to hide the key — adds latency, complexity, and is unnecessary given RLS
questionable_decisions.recommendation: Keep anon key in client per Supabase best practices. The service_role key must NEVER appear in client code — ensure .env.example only documents the anon key.

open_questions: Should the Supabase project be created in the eu-central-1 (Frankfurt) or eu-west-1 (Ireland) region? Turkey-based user latency favours Frankfurt, but eu-west-1 is on the free tier's guaranteed region list. Confirm before project creation.

Block 17 must upsert a profiles row on sign-in before any queue writes — should this be enforced by a Postgres trigger on auth.users INSERT (auto-create profile) or handled in application code? A trigger is more robust but adds schema complexity outside this block's scope.

The PRD specifies position as int4 for ordering. Should the migration also create an index on (user_id, position) for queue_items and chains to make ordered queries efficient at scale? Low impact for personal use but good practice.

Is the supabase/migrations/ directory the right location for the SQL file, or should it live at the project root as schema.sql for visibility? The migrations/ path aligns with the Supabase CLI convention but requires the CLI to be set up.
Should @supabase/supabase-js be pinned to a specific version in package.json to avoid breaking changes on npm install? Current latest is v2.x — confirm the team is tracking the v2 API (not v1).
